file_name: NETGEAR,INC_04_21_2003-EX-10.16-AMENDMENT TO THE DISTRIBUTOR AGREEMENT BETWEEN INGRAM MICRO AND NETGEAR.txt

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, including its attachment and order acknowledgments under the Agreement, constitutes the entire agreement between Distributor and NETGEAR with respect to the purchase, resale and distribution of the Products and is governed by the laws of the State of California except that body of law dealing with conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, including its attachment and order acknowledgments under the Agreement, constitutes the entire agreement between Distributor and NETGEAR with respect to the purchase, resale and distribution of the Products and is generally governed by the principles of law.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the laws of the State of California' with 'the principles of law' introduces ambiguity. 'Principles of law' is vague and could refer to various legal doctrines or common law principles, making it unclear which jurisdiction's laws apply and undermining the original contract's specified governing law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1636 states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. By replacing the specific governing law of California with the vague 'principles of law,' the modified text contradicts the requirement that the contract's interpretation adhere to the ascertainable and lawful intention of the parties under California law. This ambiguity undermines the clarity required for contract interpretation under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the specific governing law of 'the laws of the State of California' with the vague term 'the principles of law.' This introduces ambiguity regarding which jurisdiction's laws apply, undermining the original contract's specified governing law. California Civil Code Section 1636, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. The vague term in the changed text makes it difficult to ascertain the lawful intention of the parties under a specific jurisdiction, directly contradicting this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: NETGEAR reserves the right to add Products to the Price List at its sole discretion and any such additional Products shall be offered to Distributor at discounts to be determined at that time.
perturbation.changed_text: NETGEAR reserves the right to add Products to the Price List and will consider offering discounts to Distributor for such products.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be offered to Distributor at discounts to be determined at that time' to 'will consider offering discounts to Distributor for such products' makes the obligation to offer discounts discretionary. The original text implies a commitment to offer discounts, even if the specific amount is to be determined later. The modified text only commits NETGEAR to 'consider' discounts, which is much weaker and introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2-305 addresses open price terms in contracts for the sale of goods. While it allows for parties to conclude a contract even if the price is not settled, it implies an obligation to set a price in good faith. The original text indicates that discounts 'shall be offered,' implying good faith negotiation of the discount. Changing this to 'will consider offering discounts' introduces uncertainty and weakens any obligation for NETGEAR to actually negotiate discounts in good faith for new products, potentially violating the implied requirement of good faith under California Commercial Code 2-305.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2305. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2305. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) Nothing is said as to price; or (b) The price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) The price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as canceled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation for NETGEAR to offer discounts, potentially violating the implied requirement of good faith under California Commercial Code 2-305. The original text says 'shall be offered,' implying a good faith negotiation of the discount. The modified text, 'will consider offering discounts,' introduces uncertainty and weakens any obligation for NETGEAR to actually negotiate discounts in good faith for new products. Per COM 2305 (2), 'A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith.' By changing 'shall' to 'consider', it arguably introduces ambiguity that NETGEAR needs to operate in good faith, which the law implies.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall remain in effect for the current term and any renewal term of the Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, all other provisions of the Agreement remain unchanged.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall remain in effect as long as deemed mutually beneficial by both parties. Notwithstanding the foregoing, all other provisions of the Agreement remain unchanged.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the current term and any renewal term of the Agreement' with 'as long as deemed mutually beneficial by both parties' introduces significant ambiguity regarding the duration of the agreement. 'Mutually beneficial' is subjective and provides no clear criteria for determining when the amendment terminates. This makes the enforcement of the agreement's terms uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1657 specifies that if no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. By making the duration of the amendment dependent on it being 'mutually beneficial' without defining objective criteria for that determination, the amended text introduces uncertainty about its duration, and this contradicts the California law that necessitates the timeframe of any performance should be explicit in the deal. If any of the parties think that the deal is not 'mutually beneficial' anymore, then the other has to respect the interpretation. This may cause conflicts, impacting the period in the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1657. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1657. If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. If the act is in its nature capable of being done instantly—as, for example, if it consists in the payment of money only—it must be performed immediately upon the thing to be done being exactly ascertained. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a defined term for the agreement's duration ('current term and any renewal term') with a subjective one ('as long as deemed mutually beneficial'). California Civil Code Section 1657 states that 'If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed.' By introducing the 'mutually beneficial' clause without clear criteria, the agreement now lacks a specified time for performance, contradicting the requirement for a reasonable time frame as mandated by California law. The change undermines the enforceability of the agreement's duration.
